EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds were observed on the afternoon of March 3rd into the early morning hours of the 4th as the result of a low level jet accompanying a strong low pressure system. Numerous power outages were observed across northeast Kentucky during this time as trees and power lines were blown down from wind gusts as strong as 58-60 miles per hour. Once the disturbance sprinted into New England late that night, winds were able to calm down at the surface.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Grayson, numerous power outages were observed as the result of downed trees and power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon of March 25th, strong winds swept through eastern Kentucky in the wake of a cold frontal passage. Wind gusts of 40-53 mph were observed in the region during this time, with the highest being observed at the Ashland airport in Greenup County. These strong winds resulted in large trees being blown down. Once the evening arrived, the cold front and its parent low pressure system had evacuated the Central Appalachians and winds were able to return to calmer levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was blown down in Ashland Central Park.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-1 tornado passed just to the west of the Smith Mills community in Henderson County. The tornado developed along JJ Utley Road. Traveling north-northeast along Greenlick Baptist Church Road, the tornado destroyed a farm outbuilding. The tornado crossed Kentucky Highway 359 just south of Smith Mills where several trees suffered extensive large limb damage. Continuing north-northeast, the tornado passed through a cemetery along Grandpa Jones Road where several large trees were snapped. The tornado diminished as it reached Kentucky Highway 136 just west of Smith Mills. Peak winds were estimated near 95 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary front draped itself from central Texas northeast through the Ohio River Valley and produced multiple rounds of showers and thunderstorms, some with very heavy rainfall. Scattered showers and thunderstorms resulted in several cases of flooding or flash flooding. One to three inches of rain fell across the region with isolated higher amounts before the front passed east on the 25th. Moderate to heavy rainfall quickly inundated ditches and once again flooded roads around the region. Parts of the Ohio and Green Rivers rose above flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few state roads were closed by high water, including Highways 132, 138, and 270. Four other state roads were open but high water signs were placed on them. The flooding was from rain the previous day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe thunderstorms developed over western Kentucky, along and just ahead of a southeastward moving cold front. The storms formed on the northern edge of a region of strong wind shear and weak to moderate instability. The instability waned as evening approached, limiting the overall coverage of severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported dime to quarter size hail northwest and just southwest of downtown Owensboro.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe thunderstorms developed over western Kentucky, along and just ahead of a southeastward moving cold front. The storms formed on the northern edge of a region of strong wind shear and weak to moderate instability. The instability waned as evening approached, limiting the overall coverage of severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter-size hail was reported by the public and relayed through a local tv station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level storm system and intense surface low pressure moved from the Southern Plains to the Mississippi Valley during the wee morning hours of Friday, March 3rd. The surface low pressure system rapidly deepened during the day on Friday as it passed just north of the Ohio River and broke long-standing minimum pressure records at multiple locations. At Paducah, which has records dating back to 1937, the sea level pressure bottomed out at 977.7 mb, well below the previous minimum pressure record of 981 mb set in 1960. At Evansville, which has records dating back to 1897, the sea level pressure bottomed out at 976.0 mb, once again well below the 979.7 mb record observed in 1926. ||As it approached eastern Kentucky, this system's warm front spread a band of showers northward across the area overnight of the 2nd into the morning of the 3rd. Winds increased across the higher elevations above 2,500 feet and in portions of Middle Tennessee and Central Kentucky toward dawn on Friday. In the system's warm sector, winds were breezy but still relatively tame, generally gusting 25 to 50 mph, strongest on southeastern ridges and near Lake Cumberland. Many locations saw sunshine buoying temperatures to near or above 70 degrees ahead of the system's cold front which approached from the west around midday, but dew points struggled to rise above 60 even in the Lake Cumberland area. Very minimal instability and intense shear proved prohibitive to sustained shower and and thunderstorm organization with the cold front.||While the shallow showers and storms that formed failed to mix down most of the momentum within the storm system's extremely intense wind field, strong cold air advection in the low-levels resulted in an unstable low-level atmosphere behind the cold front. This allowed momentum from the intense winds just a few thousand feet above the ground to mix down and surface leading to a period of strong to damaging wind gusts, most concentrated between 5 PM and 10 PM over eastern Kentucky. Across the ridges and flatter terrain, wind gusts generally peaked between 50 and 70 mph. Winds were weaker in the more sheltered valleys and narrow hollows, peaking at 30 to 50 mph, still sufficient to bring down weaker limbs and trees. During the evening, the NWS Office at the Julian Carroll airport (KJKL) recorded multiple gusts to 58 mph or stronger, with a peak gust of 65 mph. This gust is the strongest measured gust at KJKL since records began in 1981. The strongest gust in the NWS Jackson County Warning area (CWA) recorded on Friday evening was 70 mph at the London Corbin Airport (KLOZ). While these winds were sufficient to cause structural damage, down many trees, cause scattered to widespread power outages, eastern Kentucky was spared from the destructive hurricane force wind gusts that occurred just to the west over portions of Central Kentucky and Bluegrass.||Across the Commonwealth, power outages exceeded a half million customers Friday evening, with tens of thousands of those occurring in eastern Kentucky. Significant power outages occurred in many counties including Menifee, Lee, McCreary, Wayne, Pulaski, Estill, Rockcastle, and Jackson counties. Just north and west of the region, the Lexington metro area was particularly hard hit. Power restoration crews from many nearby states including Virginia, North Carolina, South Carolina, Georgia, Tennessee, Louisiana, and Arkansas joined local utility companies in what turned into a several day power restoration effort. Many instances of loss of siding and/or roofing material were also observed. More significant structural damage reports were received from Estill and Fleming counties. Just north and west of the region, the Lexington metro area experienced stronger winds and with more widespread structural damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Kentucky Mesonet located 4 miles South of Owingsville measured a gust of 61 mph at 7pm EST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The most widespread severe thunderstorm activity in many months broke out across eastern Kentucky on Tuesday, May 16th. A warm front lifted to near the Mountain Parkway during the morning hours, ushering in a muggy airmass for those locations to the south of the Parkway. Sun shining on this juicy air mass helped build up moderate amounts of instability south of the warm front as temperatures warmed into the upper 70s to mid 80s there. Meanwhile, persistent cloud cover and a weak northeasterly breeze north of the front kept temperatures cooler and conditions much more stable. A wave of low pressure rode along the stalled warm front during this afternoon, triggering supercell storms in the area between the warm front and the Hal Rogers Parkway/KY-80 corridor. These storms dropped severe to significant-sized hail. One of the supercells also produced a brief tornado in Magoffin County southwest of Salyersville. Further south, weaker shear led to less organized storm structures, but even there the storms still generated strong outflows leading to bowing storm lines and damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Picture on social media showed approximately half dollar-sized (1.25 inch) hail a the Hazard Animal Shelter.

During the evening, the NWS Office at the Julian Carroll airport (KJKL) recorded multiple gusts to 58 mph or stronger, with a peak gust of 65 mph. This gust is the strongest measured gust at KJKL since records began in 1981. The strongest gust in the NWS Jackson County Warning area (CWA) recorded on Friday evening was 70 mph at the London Corbin Airport (KLOZ). EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved through the Ohio Valley, producing localized damaging wind gusts.
Showers and scattered thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours ahead of a strong low pressure system moving through the Ohio Valley.

The most widespread severe thunderstorm activity in many months broke out across eastern Kentucky on Tuesday, May 16th. A warm front lifted to near the Mountain Parkway during the morning hours, ushering in a muggy airmass for those locations to the south of the Parkway. Sun shining on this juicy air mass helped build up moderate amounts of instability south of the warm front as temperatures warmed into the upper 70s to mid 80s there. Meanwhile, persistent cloud cover and a weak northeasterly breeze north of the front kept temperatures cooler and conditions much more stable. A wave of low pressure rode along the stalled warm front during this afternoon, triggering supercell storms in the area between the warm front and the Hal Rogers Parkway/KY-80 corridor. These storms dropped severe to significant-sized hail. One of the supercells also produced a brief tornado in Magoffin County southwest of Salyersville. Further south, weaker shear led to less organized storm structures, but even there the storms still generated strong outflows leading to bowing storm lines and damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS Survey Team, in conjunction with Magoffin County Emergency Management, confirmed that an EF-1 Tornado touched down on a hillside southwest of Board Tree Rd, crossed the road and then traveled through the holler/drainage along Williams Arnett Cemetery Rd. It then climbed up the adjacent ridgeline before weakening and dissipating. Several structures sustained minor damage, and  many trees were snapped or uprooted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small thunderstorm complex over the Pennyrile region of western Kentucky slowed down and intensified during the morning. A very well-defined and slow-moving mesoscale convective vortex over southeast Missouri drifted off to the southeast through the morning hours, tracking into western Kentucky by midday. This system interacted with a very moist and modestly unstable air mass, resulting in heavy rainfall and isolated flash flooding. Precipitable water values remained very high, with values of 2.0 to 2.25 inches characteristic of a deeply tropical air mass. One person drowned as a result of floodwaters in west-central Christian County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was over roadways in Guthrie and Trenton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated, destructive severe thunderstorms formed during the heat of the late afternoon hours. The first storm produced very large hail up to 2.5 inches in diameter on its path from Union County to Madisonville. Another very intense storm during the early evening struck the Owensboro area with 2-inch hail and damaging winds of at least 70 mph. A third destructive storm moved eastward along the Tennessee border counties from Hickman and southern Graves Counties eastward during the early evening. The community of Wingo in Graves County received extensive damage from this storm. All of the severe storms formed in a strongly unstable atmosphere with strong wind shear ahead of a cold front moving across southeast Missouri and southern Illinois. A mid-level westerly jet stream of 50 knots and veering winds with height resulted in 45 to 55 knots of deep layer wind shear. Supercells formed along and ahead of the cold front, producing very large hail and isolated destructive winds over 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hazardous levels of heat and humidity occurred in a southwest wind flow ahead of a cold front. Heat index values peaked between 105 and 110 degrees at most locations in western Kentucky. The peak heat index values at various cities included: 110 at Henderson and Murray, 109 at Hopkinsville, 107 at Madisonville, 106 at Princeton, and 105 at Paducah. Thunderstorms erupted ahead of the cold front late in the day, bringing lower heat indices to some locations.

The peak heat index values at various cities included: 110 at Henderson and Murray, 109 at Hopkinsville, 107 at Madisonville, 106 at Princeton, and 105 at Paducah. Thunderstorms erupted ahead of the cold front late in the day, bringing lower heat indices to some locations.

Hazardous levels of heat and humidity occurred in a southwest wind flow ahead of a cold front. Heat index values peaked between 105 and 110 degrees at most locations in western Kentucky. The peak heat index values at various cities included: 110 at Henderson and Murray, 109 at Hopkinsville, 107 at Madisonville, 106 at Princeton, and 105 at Paducah. Thunderstorms erupted ahead of the cold front late in the day, bringing lower heat indices to some locations.

A well-organized thunderstorm complex tracked southward from southwest Indiana down across the Pennyrile region of western Kentucky. A couple of reports of tree damage were received with the strong winds along the leading edge of the complex. As the complex of storms took shape, it persisted long enough over the Owensboro area for significant flash flooding. This morning round of severe thunderstorms was associated with a southwest wind flow of hot and humid air ahead of a cold front that extended from Wisconsin to northwest Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At least one water rescue was conducted on the southeast side of Owensboro. A road was impassable near the Owensboro airport. A family was rescued from high water at their home just northeast of Masonville. A personal weather station in eastern Daviess County measured around 3.5 inches during the morning, much of which fell in 90 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two more rounds of severe thunderstorms affected the Owensboro area after the morning round. During the late afternoon hours, an organized squall line moved southward across northwest Kentucky. Isolated wind damage was reported, with gusts up to 60 mph at a few locations. During the early evening, an isolated severe thunderstorm tracked southeast across northwest Kentucky. This storm produced hail up to the size of quarters. The storms occurred ahead of a southeastward moving cold front that extended from northern Illinois through central Missouri. A low amplitude 500 mb shortwave overspread parts of central Illinois and Indiana during the evening, accompanied by 60 to 70 knots of mid-level wind flow. The strong wind shear supported organized storm structures, including lines and elevated supercells. Even though earlier rounds of storms stabilized the atmosphere, a strengthening low-level jet favored regeneration of convection along and atop the outflow-cooled air.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A gust front marking the leading edge of severe thunderstorms produced winds up to 60 mph across northern Daviess County. A trained spotter estimated 60 mph winds in Owensboro. Power lines were down in Maceo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm cells rapidly developed over northwest Kentucky. Large hail was the main hazard with the initial thunderstorm cells in the late afternoon hours. The cells organized into a complex of storms that moved quickly south-southeast across the Pennyrile region of west Kentucky. Strong to locally damaging winds accompanied the complex of storms. The storms formed in an extremely unstable air mass south of an outflow boundary that was generated from earlier storms well to the north. Strong daytime heating and high dew points in the mid to upper 70's yielded mixed-layer capes around 5500 j/kg. Deep-layer wind shear was very marginal for organized storm structures. Effective shear values averaged 20 to 30 knots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were down. A pole barn metal roof was partially peeled off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated, destructive severe thunderstorms formed during the heat of the late afternoon hours. The first storm produced very large hail up to 2.5 inches in diameter on its path from Union County to Madisonville. Another very intense storm during the early evening struck the Owensboro area with 2-inch hail and damaging winds of at least 70 mph. A third destructive storm moved eastward along the Tennessee border counties from Hickman and southern Graves Counties eastward during the early evening. The community of Wingo in Graves County received extensive damage from this storm. All of the severe storms formed in a strongly unstable atmosphere with strong wind shear ahead of a cold front moving across southeast Missouri and southern Illinois. A mid-level westerly jet stream of 50 knots and veering winds with height resulted in 45 to 55 knots of deep layer wind shear. Supercells formed along and ahead of the cold front, producing very large hail and isolated destructive winds over 70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail from 1.5 to 2 inches in diameter fell in a path from Sorgho to the Owensboro airport.

Excessive heat and humidity invaded western Kentucky for the first time in the 2023 summer season. Peak heat indices ranged from 115 to 120 degrees at most locations on the 30th. On the 29th, thunderstorms kept most areas east of Kentucky Lake relatively cool. However, the dangerous levels of heat and humidity encompassed the entire western Kentucky area on the 30th. Peak heat indices west of Kentucky Lake on the 29th were around 110 degrees. The highest heat indices at selected cities on the 30th were 115 at Paducah, Hopkinsville, Princeton, and Henderson, 113 at Mayfield, and 111 at Owensboro. The heat and humidity arrived on southwest winds ahead of a slow-moving cold front over Missouri and Illinois. Actual air temperatures exceeded 100 degrees in some places on the 30th, including 101 at Paducah.

The most widespread severe thunderstorm activity in many months broke out across eastern Kentucky on Tuesday, May 16th. A warm front lifted to near the Mountain Parkway during the morning hours, ushering in a muggy airmass for those locations to the south of the Parkway. Sun shining on this juicy air mass helped build up moderate amounts of instability south of the warm front as temperatures warmed into the upper 70s to mid 80s there. Meanwhile, persistent cloud cover and a weak northeasterly breeze north of the front kept temperatures cooler and conditions much more stable. A wave of low pressure rode along the stalled warm front during this afternoon, triggering supercell storms in the area between the warm front and the Hal Rogers Parkway/KY-80 corridor. These storms dropped severe to significant-sized hail. One of the supercells also produced a brief tornado in Magoffin County southwest of Salyersville. Further south, weaker shear led to less organized storm structures, but even there the storms still generated strong outflows leading to bowing storm lines and damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported nickel to quarter-sized (0.75 to 1.00 inch) hail covering the ground near Vaughns Mill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary complex of showers and thunderstorms sat over parts of western Kentucky for several hours during the evening of the 18th. The rainfall occurred along a nearly stationary frontal boundary that extended from west to east across Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A vehicle was swept 200 yards off a road into Damon Creek near the intersection of Backusburg Road and Cavett Road. The driver was rescued by a swift water rescue team. A mesonet site near Wingo in nearby Graves County measured about 3.5 inches of rain in about 3 hours and 30 minutes.

The highest heat indices at selected cities on the 30th were 115 at Paducah, Hopkinsville, Princeton, and Henderson, 113 at Mayfield, and 111 at Owensboro. The heat and humidity arrived on southwest winds ahead of a slow-moving cold front over Missouri and Illinois. Actual air temperatures exceeded 100 degrees in some places on the 30th, including 101 at Paducah.

Hazardous levels of heat and humidity occurred in a southwest wind flow ahead of a cold front. Heat index values peaked between 105 and 110 degrees at most locations in western Kentucky. The peak heat index values at various cities included: 110 at Henderson and Murray, 109 at Hopkinsville, 107 at Madisonville, 106 at Princeton, and 105 at Paducah. Thunderstorms erupted ahead of the cold front late in the day, bringing lower heat indices to some locations.

Isolated, destructive severe thunderstorms formed during the heat of the late afternoon hours. The first storm produced very large hail up to 2.5 inches in diameter on its path from Union County to Madisonville. Another very intense storm during the early evening struck the Owensboro area with 2-inch hail and damaging winds of at least 70 mph. A third destructive storm moved eastward along the Tennessee border counties from Hickman and southern Graves Counties eastward during the early evening. The community of Wingo in Graves County received extensive damage from this storm. All of the severe storms formed in a strongly unstable atmosphere with strong wind shear ahead of a cold front moving across southeast Missouri and southern Illinois. A mid-level westerly jet stream of 50 knots and veering winds with height resulted in 45 to 55 knots of deep layer wind shear. Supercells formed along and ahead of the cold front, producing very large hail and isolated destructive winds over 70 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were down from Stanley to Owensboro and across much of the rest of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The most widespread severe thunderstorm activity in many months broke out across eastern Kentucky on Tuesday, May 16th. A warm front lifted to near the Mountain Parkway during the morning hours, ushering in a muggy airmass for those locations to the south of the Parkway. Sun shining on this juicy air mass helped build up moderate amounts of instability south of the warm front as temperatures warmed into the upper 70s to mid 80s there. Meanwhile, persistent cloud cover and a weak northeasterly breeze north of the front kept temperatures cooler and conditions much more stable. A wave of low pressure rode along the stalled warm front during this afternoon, triggering supercell storms in the area between the warm front and the Hal Rogers Parkway/KY-80 corridor. These storms dropped severe to significant-sized hail. One of the supercells also produced a brief tornado in Magoffin County southwest of Salyersville. Further south, weaker shear led to less organized storm structures, but even there the storms still generated strong outflows leading to bowing storm lines and damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County dispatch reported tree blown down along KY-1505 near the intersection of KY-1326.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread major damage to trees and power lines was reported in a path across southern Graves County. The area from Wingo to Sedalia was in the most concentrated damage path. Trees were down across numerous roads. Power poles were snapped in the Wingo area. A garage was blown off its foundation near Wingo. A jeep was moved about 40 feet and pushed into a fence. Shingles were blown off houses. Trees landed on houses and vehicles. An empty school bus was demolished. About 1,000 utility customers were still without power the following morning, mostly in the Wingo area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The roof was torn off a pole barn, minor shingle damage occurred to a home, and trees were down on a farm 3.5 miles north of Trenton. This damage was very close to the observed 95 mph wind gust from a mesonet site. The Todd County mesonet site located 5 miles southwest of Elkton measured a wind gust to 95 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving cold front plodded southeastward into western Kentucky during the evening hours of June 25th. Thunderstorms developed along this boundary across the Lower Ohio Valley, grew upscale and became a cold-pool driven line with embedded supercells. These storms dropped southeast across Central Kentucky through a high instability environment before encountering a more stable air mass east of the Pottsville Escarpment. This led to a gradual weakening of the line of storms once they moved into the Eastern Kentucky Coalfields but not before producing dozens of wind damage reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public reported two trees blown down at a residence near Lerose.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front was parked across western Kentucky during the early morning hours on Sunday June 11th. This slowly transitioned eastward through the first part of the day as an area of low pressure tracked along the Ohio River. By late afternoon and evening, the warm front was through eastern Kentucky, putting us in a more unstable pattern, especially with a quickly approaching cold front and a low pressure system so close by. Multiple Severe Thunderstorm and Tornado Warnings were issued between 4pm and 9pm across eastern Kentucky, with widespread reports of damaging winds and large hail from some of the storms that developed. The strongest storm impacted Pulaski county, resulting in baseball size hail between Nancy and Somerset, and widespread straight-line winds in the communities of Faubush and Nancy. Activity finally settled down as we headed into the overnight an lost the best instability, though the cold front didn't move through until the wee hours of the morning on the 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Just a few minutes after posting a report of 1.00 inch hail in the vicinity of Cumberland Lake and Pulaski County Park, the hail size increased to 1.5 inches (ping pong ball size).

By late afternoon and evening, the warm front was through eastern Kentucky, putting us in a more unstable pattern, especially with a quickly approaching cold front and a low pressure system so close by. Multiple Severe Thunderstorm and Tornado Warnings were issued between 4pm and 9pm across eastern Kentucky, with widespread reports of damaging winds and large hail from some of the storms that developed. The strongest storm impacted Pulaski county, resulting in baseball size hail between Nancy and Somerset, and widespread straight-line winds in the communities of Faubush and Nancy. Activity finally settled down as we headed into the overnight an lost the best instability, though the cold front didn't move through until the wee hours of the morning on the 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were reported to have been blown down in and near the community of Livingston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving cold front plodded southeastward into western Kentucky during the evening hours of June 25th. Thunderstorms developed along this boundary across the Lower Ohio Valley, grew upscale and became a cold-pool driven line with embedded supercells. These storms dropped southeast across Central Kentucky through a high instability environment before encountering a more stable air mass east of the Pottsville Escarpment. This led to a gradual weakening of the line of storms once they moved into the Eastern Kentucky Coalfields but not before producing dozens of wind damage reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public reported multiple trees blown down in the Bugwood community.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak surface trough sagged south of I-64 during the evening hours of June 26th, providing a focus for showers and storms to fire late in the day.  This activity coalesced into a loosely organized line toward sunset. Portions of the line produced winds strong enough to produce isolated wind damage reports in Morgan County before settling further southeast and dissipating overnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County dispatch reported a tree blown down about 2.5 miles up KY-650.

Activity finally settled down as we headed into the overnight an lost the best instability, though the cold front didn't move through until the wee hours of the morning on the 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media pictures shared from near the Lees Ford Marina show hail the size of golf balls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small thunderstorm complex over the Pennyrile region of western Kentucky slowed down and intensified during the morning. A very well-defined and slow-moving mesoscale convective vortex over southeast Missouri drifted off to the southeast through the morning hours, tracking into western Kentucky by midday. This system interacted with a very moist and modestly unstable air mass, resulting in heavy rainfall and isolated flash flooding. Precipitable water values remained very high, with values of 2.0 to 2.25 inches characteristic of a deeply tropical air mass. One person drowned as a result of floodwaters in west-central Christian County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads were flooded in Princeton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front was parked across western Kentucky during the early morning hours on Sunday June 11th. This slowly transitioned eastward through the first part of the day as an area of low pressure tracked along the Ohio River. By late afternoon and evening, the warm front was through eastern Kentucky, putting us in a more unstable pattern, especially with a quickly approaching cold front and a low pressure system so close by. Multiple Severe Thunderstorm and Tornado Warnings were issued between 4pm and 9pm across eastern Kentucky, with widespread reports of damaging winds and large hail from some of the storms that developed. The strongest storm impacted Pulaski county, resulting in baseball size hail between Nancy and Somerset, and widespread straight-line winds in the communities of Faubush and Nancy. Activity finally settled down as we headed into the overnight an lost the best instability, though the cold front didn't move through until the wee hours of the morning on the 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media video shared with local media shows hail around golf ball size falling on the west side of Somerset.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving cold front plodded southeastward into western Kentucky during the evening hours of June 25th. Thunderstorms developed along this boundary across the Lower Ohio Valley, grew upscale and became a cold-pool driven line with embedded supercells. These storms dropped southeast across Central Kentucky through a high instability environment before encountering a more stable air mass east of the Pottsville Escarpment. This led to a gradual weakening of the line of storms once they moved into the Eastern Kentucky Coalfields but not before producing dozens of wind damage reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County dispatch reported trees blown down onto Winston Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusual pattern set up was in place on Saturday June 3, 2022. A cold front was dropping southwest into the state from the northeast, resulting in strong storms along the boundary in the far eastern portion of the state and moving westward instead of the usual eastward direction. The unstable air led to shower and storm development, with some of the strongest storms setting up across Pike County. Overall these storms led to multiple reports of tree damage across the county. Martin County also received a tree down due to an outflow boundary from the larger storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported to have been blown down at the 1600 block of Old Ferrells Creek Road, east of the community of Belcher.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously strong mid- and upper-level trough for late June moved across the Great Lakes and Ohio Valley from the afternoon hours on June 25th through the morning hours on June 26th. A 60-70 kt 500 mb jet bisected the mid-Mississippi Valley, placing central Kentucky and southern Indiana in the left exit region, contributing to broad rising motion over the area. Near the surface, a well-defined cold front crossed the Mississippi River during the mid-to-late afternoon hours on June 25th, with temperatures in the upper 80s and lower 90s and dewpoints in the upper 60s and lower 70s in the warm sector ahead of the front. In the warm sector, mixed-layer CAPE of 2500-3500 J/kg provided plenty of fuel for convective development aside from the aforementioned favorable jet forcing. With 25-35 knots of flow at 850 mb, there was also ample vertical wind shear across the region, with effective bulk shear values generally above 45 kt, leading to the development of supercellular structures. The parameter space was favorable for all severe hazards. 0-1 km storm-relative helicity was over 200 m2/s2 and significant tornado parameter values were frequently greater than 1, suggesting the potential for rotating mesocyclones and tornadoes. Downdraft CAPE was over 1200 J/kg along and west of Interstate 65, suggesting the potential for strong winds from any formidable downdrafts. Finally, sufficient mid-level lapse rates and significant hail parameter values warranted concerns for severe hail. As would be expected given the parameter space, all severe hazards were observed, with a couple tornadoes and numerous straight-line wind and hail reports across southern Indiana and central Kentucky. Straight-line winds of up to 100 mph and multiple reports of golf ball or larger size hail serve as evidence of the highly unusual setup across the Ohio and Tennessee valleys June 25th and 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gaither Station Road was closed due to water over the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously strong mid- and upper-level trough for late June moved across the Great Lakes and Ohio Valley from the afternoon hours on June 25th through the morning hours on June 26th. A 60-70 kt 500 mb jet bisected the mid-Mississippi Valley, placing central Kentucky and southern Indiana in the left exit region, contributing to broad rising motion over the area. Near the surface, a well-defined cold front crossed the Mississippi River during the mid-to-late afternoon hours on June 25th, with temperatures in the upper 80s and lower 90s and dewpoints in the upper 60s and lower 70s in the warm sector ahead of the front. In the warm sector, mixed-layer CAPE of 2500-3500 J/kg provided plenty of fuel for convective development aside from the aforementioned favorable jet forcing. With 25-35 knots of flow at 850 mb, there was also ample vertical wind shear across the region, with effective bulk shear values generally above 45 kt, leading to the development of supercellular structures. Downdraft CAPE was over 1200 J/kg along and west of Interstate 65, suggesting the potential for strong winds from any formidable downdrafts. Finally, sufficient mid-level lapse rates and significant hail parameter values warranted concerns for severe hail. As would be expected given the parameter space, all severe hazards were observed, with a couple tornadoes and numerous straight-line wind and hail reports across southern Indiana and central Kentucky. Straight-line winds of up to 100 mph and multiple reports of golf ball or larger size hail serve as evidence of the highly unusual setup across the Ohio and Tennessee valleys June 25th and 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was reported near Hillview.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a cold front dropped southeast through the Lower Ohio Valley, two lines of enhanced convection moved through central Kentucky. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down just east of US 127, north of Woolen Mill Road, in an area of mostly open fields with patches of trees along fence rows. Damage could be seen in the tree tops lowering until reaching the ground. The tornado moved eastward towards |West Cumberland Avenue in western Jamestown. While plowing through clusters of trees, tree damage was highly focused with many mature healthy trees being snapped. As the tornado approached a set of apartment buildings on the 1100 block of West Cumberland Avenue, views from an aerial drone showed heavy tree damage with trees falling in several directions. A roof from one of the buildings blew off to a southwest direction. A large window from across the street at the auto museum got sucked out and ended up in the roadway. To the north, large sections of the roof at Jamestown Auto Sales got peeled back from the northwest to the southeast. ||The main tornado track continued east while a small suction vortex fed into the main rotation near Telephone Drive. Along Telephone Drive, a large tree was uprooted and fell to the southeast while two well built buildings had their gable porch roofs blown to the north. One remained attached to the roof while the other was blown to the northeast over the building, coming to rest behind C&W Auto Parts which had its own |southeast facing window blown out. All three of these buildings had mud splattered on their southeast facing exterior wall. ||Farther east in the main tornado track, the tornado damaged a large area of the Carpet Center's roof. Just to the south, a single family home had half of its roof blown off. To the north and east of the Carpet Center, the strongest damage was done at a residence. A garage made out of cinder blocks was completely leveled by 110 mph winds on two sides while the other two sides saw nothing remain above the windows. Every tree in the area was severely damaged. Multiple trees fell on the house and the |owners vehicles. A limb of a tree punched through the wall and was in the house. Multiple large trees continued to get uprooted as the tornado traveled eastward before lifting near Brown Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Weather Service in Louisville conducted a storm survey for the macroburst damage extending from western Grayson County, through Edmonson County, before ending in northeastern Warren County. The first area of concentrated damage was observed along US Highway 62 around Caneyville, which consisted mostly of softwood and hardwood tree damage, including numerous uprooted trees and large snapped branches. One double wide trailer suffered considerable roof damage in along Highway 62, though structural damage was otherwise rare in this area. Estimated winds across western Grayson County were generally between 80 and 90 mph. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously strong mid- and upper-level trough for late June moved across the Great Lakes and Ohio Valley from the afternoon hours on June 25th through the morning hours on June 26th. A 60-70 kt 500 mb jet bisected the mid-Mississippi Valley, placing central Kentucky and southern Indiana in the left exit region, contributing to broad rising motion over the area. Near the surface, a well-defined cold front crossed the Mississippi River during the mid-to-late afternoon hours on June 25th, with temperatures in the upper 80s and lower 90s and dewpoints in the upper 60s and lower 70s in the warm sector ahead of the front. In the warm sector, mixed-layer CAPE of 2500-3500 J/kg provided plenty of fuel for convective development aside from the aforementioned favorable jet forcing. With 25-35 knots of flow at 850 mb, there was also ample vertical wind shear across the region, with effective bulk shear values generally above 45 kt, leading to the development of supercellular structures. The parameter space was favorable for all severe hazards. 0-1 km storm-relative helicity was over 200 m2/s2 and significant tornado parameter values were frequently greater than 1, suggesting the potential for rotating mesocyclones and tornadoes. Downdraft CAPE was over 1200 J/kg along and west of Interstate 65, suggesting the potential for strong winds from any formidable downdrafts. Finally, sufficient mid-level lapse rates and significant hail parameter values warranted concerns for severe hail. As would be expected given the parameter space, all severe hazards were observed, with a couple tornadoes and numerous straight-line wind and hail reports across southern Indiana and central Kentucky. Straight-line winds of up to 100 mph and multiple reports of golf ball or larger size hail serve as evidence of the highly unusual setup across the Ohio and Tennessee valleys June 25th and 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was observed in Caneyville.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A narrow, but powerful, EF-2 tornado developed quickly in the Cecilia area late Sunday evening, as a cluster of thunderstorms briefly interacted with an east-west oriented outflow boundary from earlier storm activity.  The storm first caused tree damage along Long Grove Rd near Tabb Rd. A property near this intersection had several trees uprooted and snapped as well as limbs down. On the south side of Tabb Rd. roofing material from the northern side landed on that property. An RV that had been along a row of trees south of Tabb Rd was forced onto its side as well.||The tornado continued southeast across cropland. Drone footage indicated a nice swath of damage in the corn. The tornado then affected two homes on the end of Basham Lane.  The first house had a carport thrown to the right of the track into an open field. An AC unit was flipped on its side at the house and a brick facade wall came off the house structure. Farther southeast, the strongest winds occurred as the tornado pulled up the wide front porch of a house and then peeled the roof back. Parts of the roof landed upstream of where the tornado caused the damage.  A gazebo behind the house was thrown in a counter clockwise fashion back to the other side of the house. Two parked trucks were shifted from their location as well.  Damage continued in the corn field southeast of property, with drone footage indicating some jagged movement of the tornado on the ground.||It continued just past another row of trees where it impacted a barn before the tornado lifted.||The survey drove to several other sites across the county...along Black Branch, Hayden School, Rineyville, and Locust Grove roads. The rest of the damage had trees falling generally toward the south, in line with straight lined winds on the order of 80-90 mph.

Across the road on Lily Creek Ramp Road, Cumberland Boat Storage had at least 9 bays of their western most building completely destroyed. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Weather Service conducted a storm survey for the macroburst damage received near Richmond in Madison county, Kentucky. Damage was first observed along US Highway 25 where some isolated tree snaps occurred. Plenty of tree damage continued to Hunter Lane and extensive property damage occurred to a home where roof damage and damage to outbuildings occurred.||The storm continued along Four Mile Road where damage to a barn roof occurred with more trees uprooted and snapped. Damage continued to become more sporadic along Greens Crossing Road where lots of tree damage and barn outbuildings had roof damage. Richmond Speedway also received roof damage to the restrooms and grandstand seating. The most concentrated damage occurred along Forest and Kennedy Lanes where several residents received damage with very extensive tree damage, including a mixture of uprooted and snapped hardwood trees. Several barn outbuildings also received considerable roof damage. Observable damage continued with trees down along Whitt Road and Speedwell Road and some barn roof damage. Barn outbuildings and tree damage continued toward Brassfield Road before the main extensive damage ended. A majority of the damage was observed in a southerly direction, although some damage varied between the easterly and southwesterly directions. This damage pattern coincides with a macroburst style severe straight line wind downburst from the parent thunderstorm that moved across Madison County. Damage was widespread and appeared to even fan out in many areas, which is also consistent with a straight line wind event. Most damage was consistently in the 90-100 mph range with a max wind speed of 110 in some locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously strong mid- and upper-level trough for late June moved across the Great Lakes and Ohio Valley from the afternoon hours on June 25th through the morning hours on June 26th. A 60-70 kt 500 mb jet bisected the mid-Mississippi Valley, placing central Kentucky and southern Indiana in the left exit region, contributing to broad rising motion over the area. Near the surface, a well-defined cold front crossed the Mississippi River during the mid-to-late afternoon hours on June 25th, with temperatures in the upper 80s and lower 90s and dewpoints in the upper 60s and lower 70s in the warm sector ahead of the front. In the warm sector, mixed-layer CAPE of 2500-3500 J/kg provided plenty of fuel for convective development aside from the aforementioned favorable jet forcing. With 25-35 knots of flow at 850 mb, there was also ample vertical wind shear across the region, with effective bulk shear values generally above 45 kt, leading to the development of supercellular structures. The parameter space was favorable for all severe hazards. 0-1 km storm-relative helicity was over 200 m2/s2 and significant tornado parameter values were frequently greater than 1, suggesting the potential for rotating mesocyclones and tornadoes. Downdraft CAPE was over 1200 J/kg along and west of Interstate 65, suggesting the potential for strong winds from any formidable downdrafts. Finally, sufficient mid-level lapse rates and significant hail parameter values warranted concerns for severe hail. As would be expected given the parameter space, all severe hazards were observed, with a couple tornadoes and numerous straight-line wind and hail reports across southern Indiana and central Kentucky. Straight-line winds of up to 100 mph and multiple reports of golf ball or larger size hail serve as evidence of the highly unusual setup across the Ohio and Tennessee valleys June 25th and 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees had tree top damage with large limbs broken. A tree fell onto a house on Rineyville-Big Springs Road, producing gutter and minor roof damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface high pressure was over the Lower Ohio Valley, but with an upper ridge centered over the CONUS, northwest flow moved several waves of convection through central Kentucky. This resulted in multiple wind damage and hail reports with limited flash flooding reports. A barn fire was also started by a lightning strike.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was identified around Riverview Drive and Holland Avenue. There were also flooding issues on US 60 about 1 mile east of Lewisport.

A couple of lines of thunderstorms moved northeast across far western Kentucky during the late afternoon and evening hours. The first line consisted of semi-discrete cells, one of which was associated with a weak tornado that tracked across the Kentucky Lake area. The second line was associated with a few isolated marginally severe wind gusts to 60 mph. The storms developed in response to a rather amplified 500 mb shortwave trough that progressed east into Iowa and Missouri by nightfall. With daytime heating, the atmosphere became very unstable in the afternoon. Mixed-layer capes were around 3000 j/kg late in the day. A belt of 35 to 45 knot mid-level west-southwesterly winds resulted in sufficient deep-layer wind shear for organized storm structures. An embedded tornado occurred in the vicinity of a residual outflow boundary arcing west from storms over central Kentucky.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line developed over southeast Missouri and moved rapidly east-southeast across western Kentucky. Areas along and southwest of a line from Hopkinsville to Princeton to Smithland received widespread strong winds. Locally damaging winds occurred, especially from Ballard County southeast across the Paducah, Mayfield, and Kentucky Lake regions to Hopkinsville. The bow-shaped squall line formed from a cluster of thunderstorms over central Missouri during the late morning hours. South of a surface front that extended from central Missouri to the I-64 corridor of southern Illinois, rapid heating of a moist air mass produced strong instability. Mixed layer cape values climbed above 3000 j/kg. A core of strong mid-level winds from 50 to 60 knots in the vicinity of the storms aided in rapid propagation and organization of the storms across western Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few miles northeast of Wickliffe, multiple trees were down, and windows were blown out of a house. Trees were down at several other locations in the county, including Bandana.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approached Kentucky from the northwest overnight, and passed through the state during the afternoon and evening hours. An MCS that had developed along the front the previous day dropped southeastward overnight, with a defined outflow boundary on the WPC surface maps. These initial storms led to four warnings, and one report of wind damage from an NWS employee. The main action occurred later in the day, as eastern Kentucky found itself in a moist and unstable airmass just ahead of the incoming cold front. Most the the Jackson, KY warning forecast area was warned at some point, with the main impact being damaging winds as most of the storms formed into bowing segments with strongest winds along the front edge. Numerous reports of trees down were made across multiple counties through the early and mid-afternoon hours, before the quick-moving system pushed southeast out of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were blown down along Browns Fork Road just southwest of Hazard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A bowing line of strong to severe thunderstorms moved rapidly southeast from southern Illinois across far western Kentucky. This line was associated with numerous pockets of wind damage. The line of storms moved through a strongly unstable air mass during the heat of the day, when mixed-layer capes were as high as 3000 j/kg. A similar airmass extended east into western Kentucky, which helped the storms maintain organization as they moved east-southeastward through the lower Ohio Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell through the roof of an apartment in Reidland. A tree limb fell on a power line near Hendron.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm, muggy and unstable air mass was in place across eastern Kentucky on July 18th as a wavering front remained northwest of the Lower Ohio River. The first storm cluster of the day fired over central Kentucky during the early afternoon hours before moving into eastern Kentucky. This initial round of storms impacted areas primarily north of the Hal Rogers Parkway during the late afternoon and early evening. The combination of dry air in the mid-levels and sufficient shear led to a a couple of strong bowing line segments with estimated peak wind gusts in the 60 to 70 mph range. The most numerous instances of wind damage, primarily downed trees and power lines, were observed in Estill, Lee, Montgomery and Bath counties. A gust of 61 mph was recorded at the RAWS site close to the Jackson National Weather Service Office. A second larger convective system dropped into the the Commonwealth during the mid-afternoon but then decayed as it moved onto the Cumberland Plateau. However, some cells with this system will still strong enough to bring a few instances of severe wind damage to portions of southcentral Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS employees at WFO Jackson estimated wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph at times for an approximately 5 minute period from 5:03 PM to 5:08 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of intense thunderstorm cells rapidly formed in a strongly unstable air mass during the heat of the afternoon. The most intense cells merged into a small severe thunderstorm complex around the northern end of the Land Between The Lakes area (Kentucky Lake and Lake Barkley). This complex was associated with sporadic large hail and damaging winds from southern Livingston County eastward across Lyon and Caldwell Counties. The storms occurred ahead of a slow-moving weak cold front that extended from central Indiana to the Ozarks of southwest Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported 1.5-inch hail at Ledbetter. The public reported golf ball size hail at Smithland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing upper level disturbance with additional contribution from preceding hot and muggy conditions led to strong to severe thunderstorms on the afternoon of July 18th. In the span of around 45 minutes, damaging wind gusts swept through Greenup County and led to numerous areas of downed trees. The quick pace of storms allowed activity to quiet down quickly in eastern Kentucky as convection pressed eastward.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down along Beechy Creek near the Beechy township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed in eastern Kentucky on the afternoon of July 13th in the midst of weak disturbances passing through the area. Abundant moisture was in place during this time with surface dew points above 70 degrees, which allowed storms to thrive during the daylight hours. Strong wind gusts accompanying storms resulted in downed trees around Greenup, one of which fell onto a local home. Storms tapered down with the loss of daytime heating after sunset.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell onto a house and nearby power lines along State Route 207 near Baker Cemetery Road in Greenup. Several other trees fell around the Greenup area as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Aside from far eastern Kentucky (roughly US-23 and eastward) where showers and storms lingered through much of the morning, the combination of dew points in the mid 70s to lower 80s and temperatures climbing well into the 80s and lower 90s led to an oppressively muggy and highly unstable environment by the midday hours of the 25th. Thunderstorms began to fire off by early afternoon near the I-64 corridor. These storms struggled to organize due to limited shear and lack of focusing mechanism. However, the stronger storms still produced multiple instances of severe wind gust damage, particularly over Morgan, Menifee, Powell and Estill counties. The thunderstorms also produced a considerable amount of lightning, one instance of which caused the death of a 39-year old male in Bath County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down near the intersection of Woody Ware and Pompeii roads.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down near 100 Wells Street in Clay City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm, muggy and unstable air mass was in place across eastern Kentucky on July 18th as a wavering front remained northwest of the Lower Ohio River. The first storm cluster of the day fired over central Kentucky during the early afternoon hours before moving into eastern Kentucky. This initial round of storms impacted areas primarily north of the Hal Rogers Parkway during the late afternoon and early evening. The combination of dry air in the mid-levels and sufficient shear led to a a couple of strong bowing line segments with estimated peak wind gusts in the 60 to 70 mph range. The most numerous instances of wind damage, primarily downed trees and power lines, were observed in Estill, Lee, Montgomery and Bath counties. A gust of 61 mph was recorded at the RAWS site close to the Jackson National Weather Service Office. A second larger convective system dropped into the the Commonwealth during the mid-afternoon but then decayed as it moved onto the Cumberland Plateau. However, some cells with this system will still strong enough to bring a few instances of severe wind damage to portions of southcentral Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down along SE Licking River Road near the intersection with Higgins Branch Road just south of Royalton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line developed over southeast Missouri and moved rapidly east-southeast across western Kentucky. Areas along and southwest of a line from Hopkinsville to Princeton to Smithland received widespread strong winds. Locally damaging winds occurred, especially from Ballard County southeast across the Paducah, Mayfield, and Kentucky Lake regions to Hopkinsville. The bow-shaped squall line formed from a cluster of thunderstorms over central Missouri during the late morning hours. South of a surface front that extended from central Missouri to the I-64 corridor of southern Illinois, rapid heating of a moist air mass produced strong instability. Mixed layer cape values climbed above 3000 j/kg. A core of strong mid-level winds from 50 to 60 knots in the vicinity of the storms aided in rapid propagation and organization of the storms across western Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were down in town. At least one home was damaged by downed trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small, isolated thunderstorm cell produced damaging winds over a very small area in Hopkinsville. The storm occurred in hot, unstable air ahead of a cold front over southeast Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was wind damage to several roofs in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of intense thunderstorm cells rapidly formed in a strongly unstable air mass during the heat of the afternoon. The most intense cells merged into a small severe thunderstorm complex around the northern end of the Land Between The Lakes area (Kentucky Lake and Lake Barkley). This complex was associated with sporadic large hail and damaging winds from southern Livingston County eastward across Lyon and Caldwell Counties. The storms occurred ahead of a slow-moving weak cold front that extended from central Indiana to the Ozarks of southwest Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to 58 mph was recorded at the Kentucky mesonet site near Princeton. There were multiple reports of trees down in Princeton. Trees were down over Highway 903 four miles south-southwest of Princeton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A heat wave brought three consecutive days of heat indices near or above 105 degrees. Excessive heat occurred over the Henderson/Owensboro region on the afternoons of the 27th and 28th, when heat indices peaked mostly from 109 to 115 degrees. Elsewhere, peak heat indices ranged mostly from 105 to 110 degrees on the 27th and 28th. On the 29th, the highest heat indices were from 100 to near 110 across west Kentucky. The heat and humidity occurred in a southwest wind flow ahead of a cold front that progressed southeast from the Plains over the three-day period.

A passing upper level disturbance with additional contribution from preceding hot and muggy conditions led to strong to severe thunderstorms on the afternoon of July 18th. In the span of around 45 minutes, damaging wind gusts swept through Greenup County and led to numerous areas of downed trees. The quick pace of storms allowed activity to quiet down quickly in eastern Kentucky as convection pressed eastward.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Downed trees in Greenup resulted in compromised power lines, which resulted in local customers to be without power for a brief period of time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Aside from far eastern Kentucky (roughly US-23 and eastward) where showers and storms lingered through much of the morning, the combination of dew points in the mid 70s to lower 80s and temperatures climbing well into the 80s and lower 90s led to an oppressively muggy and highly unstable environment by the midday hours of the 25th. Thunderstorms began to fire off by early afternoon near the I-64 corridor. These storms struggled to organize due to limited shear and lack of focusing mechanism. However, the stronger storms still produced multiple instances of severe wind gust damage, particularly over Morgan, Menifee, Powell and Estill counties. The thunderstorms also produced a considerable amount of lightning, one instance of which caused the death of a 39-year old male in Bath County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two trees were blown down near 270 Sub Station a few miles west of West Irvine.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lone strong thunderstorm cluster developed over Menifee County ahead of a cold front during the late afternoon of the 26th. As the storm moved southeast, it briefly became severe and produced wind damage in at least two locations in Breathitt County. The cluster later dissipated as it moved deeper into Southeast Kentucky during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down along KY-1812 near the top of Panbowl Hill, approximately 2.5 miles from the intersection of KY-1812 and KY-15.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A bowing line of strong to severe thunderstorms moved rapidly southeast from southern Illinois across far western Kentucky. This line was associated with numerous pockets of wind damage. The line of storms moved through a strongly unstable air mass during the heat of the day, when mixed-layer capes were as high as 3000 j/kg. A similar airmass extended east into western Kentucky, which helped the storms maintain organization as they moved east-southeastward through the lower Ohio Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree limbs were blown down.

An isolated severe thunderstorm quickly formed over northwest Kentucky in the heat of the afternoon. The storm pulsed up to severe levels for a brief time, with damaging winds downing trees on houses. The storm formed ahead of a cold front that trailed southwest across central Missouri from a low pressure center over the Great Lakes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A microburst occurred on U.S. Highway 41 in the Robards area, then spread westward a short distance on Highway 416. Three trees were down on houses, and one tree was down on a car.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A bowing line of strong to severe thunderstorms moved rapidly southeast from southern Illinois across far western Kentucky. This line was associated with numerous pockets of wind damage. The line of storms moved through a strongly unstable air mass during the heat of the day, when mixed-layer capes were as high as 3000 j/kg. A similar airmass extended east into western Kentucky, which helped the storms maintain organization as they moved east-southeastward through the lower Ohio Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell on a house in Symsonia, trapping a 75-year-old male. He later succumbed to his injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Historic flash flooding struck parts of far western Kentucky. Among the hardest hit counties was Graves County, which had not completely recovered from a catastrophic tornado in December of 2021. The city of Mayfield, which bore the brunt of the December 2021 tornado, experienced catastrophic flooding that inundated dozens of homes, businesses, schools, and various other institutions. The governor of Kentucky visited the city of Mayfield to tour the damage. Some smaller communities suffered catastrophic damage, such as Arlington in Carlisle County, where multiple homes were flooded. Swiftly moving water through Arlington prompted water rescues from emergency services. Widespread flash flooding of roads made travel difficult or impossible for several hours during the peak of the heaviest rainfall in the morning. Among the most impactful road closures was Interstate 69 in southwest Graves County, near the community of Wingo. Other high-profile road closures included U.S. Highways 51, 62, and 45 at various locations in Ballard, McCracken, Graves, and Hickman Counties. Numerous water rescues were conducted for people stranded in homes and vehicles. Dozens of roads were washed out in several counties, including Ballard, Carlisle, Hickman, Fulton, and Graves. Swift water was across many roads, and water even overtopped a few bridges, including U.S. 60 between Paducah and Kevil. Crop damage was expected to run into the millions of dollars. At one farm alone in southern Graves County, crop damage was estimated around 40,000 dollars. The downtown area of Fulton was flooded, including at least one business. Widespread street flooding occurred in Murray, and at least one business was sandbagged. About a dozen businesses were flooded in Ballard County, along with another dozen residences. Widespread rainfall totals ranging from 6 to 12 inches occurred over the westernmost counties (west of Kentucky Lake), including the Paducah, Mayfield, and Murray areas. The majority of this rain fell within a 10-hour window between midnight and 10 AM. The highest confirmed rainfall total was 12.76 inches in Graves County, west of Mayfield near the small community of Fancy Farm. The Kentucky mesonet site six miles southwest of Mayfield received 11.28 of rain in a 24-hour period. Preliminary data indicates this broke the previous Kentucky state record for 24-hour rainfall. The old record was 10.48 in Louisville, KY back on March 1, 1997.||In addition to all the flooding, there were several reports of trees blown down across roads. A couple of large hail reports were also received.||The historic rainfall event was caused by a persistent thunderstorm complex that continually regenerated as it moved southeast across the region during the early morning hours of the 19th. This complex of thunderstorms was indirectly caused by the complex of severe thunderstorms during the midday hours of the 18th. The complex on the 18th left an outflow boundary from south central Missouri into northeast Arkansas. This boundary lifted back northeast into southeast Missouri during the overnight and early morning hours of July 19th. At the same time, a southwest low-level jet ramped up over the top of the surface boundary. Extremely high moisture levels were present, with precipitable water values up to 2.3 inches. The position of the boundary oriented parallel with the upper-level wind flow provided an ideal setup for training convection over the same areas. The initial storms blew up by 11 PM and continued to channel southeastward across the same areas through the following morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Aside from far eastern Kentucky (roughly US-23 and eastward) where showers and storms lingered through much of the morning, the combination of dew points in the mid 70s to lower 80s and temperatures climbing well into the 80s and lower 90s led to an oppressively muggy and highly unstable environment by the midday hours of the 25th. Thunderstorms began to fire off by early afternoon near the I-64 corridor. These storms struggled to organize due to limited shear and lack of focusing mechanism. However, the stronger storms still produced multiple instances of severe wind gust damage, particularly over Morgan, Menifee, Powell and Estill counties. The thunderstorms also produced a considerable amount of lightning, one instance of which caused the death of a 39-year old male in Bath County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down near 100 Wall Street in North Irvine.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm, muggy and unstable air mass was in place across eastern Kentucky on July 18th as a wavering front remained northwest of the Lower Ohio River. The first storm cluster of the day fired over central Kentucky during the early afternoon hours before moving into eastern Kentucky. This initial round of storms impacted areas primarily north of the Hal Rogers Parkway during the late afternoon and early evening. The combination of dry air in the mid-levels and sufficient shear led to a a couple of strong bowing line segments with estimated peak wind gusts in the 60 to 70 mph range. The most numerous instances of wind damage, primarily downed trees and power lines, were observed in Estill, Lee, Montgomery and Bath counties. A gust of 61 mph was recorded at the RAWS site close to the Jackson National Weather Service Office. A second larger convective system dropped into the the Commonwealth during the mid-afternoon but then decayed as it moved onto the Cumberland Plateau. However, some cells with this system will still strong enough to bring a few instances of severe wind damage to portions of southcentral Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down onto powerlines near the 160 Southland Drive on the south side of the city of London.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms developed over the Lower Ohio Valley during the mid-afternoon hours of the 24th with a few dozen instances of wind damage being reported from Southern Ohio back into far eastern Missouri. As those storms settled southeast into the foothills of eastern Kentucky during the mid and late evening, they encountered a more stable environment and weakened. However, a one robust storm cell was still strong enough to produce two instances of wind damage in Bath County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reportedly blown down blocking Rooster Run Road southeast of the community of Bethel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small complex of thunderstorms tracked eastward across western Kentucky during the early evening hours. Sporadic tree damage and large hail accompanied the strongest storms, most of which followed a west-to-east corridor through Paducah to Princeton. The storm occurred ahead of a cold front that trailed southwest from the Great Lakes across central Missouri.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A bowing line of strong to severe thunderstorms moved rapidly southeast from southern Illinois across far western Kentucky. This line was associated with numerous pockets of wind damage. The line of storms moved through a strongly unstable air mass during the heat of the day, when mixed-layer capes were as high as 3000 j/kg. A similar airmass extended east into western Kentucky, which helped the storms maintain organization as they moved east-southeastward through the lower Ohio Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The mesonet site near Princeton measured a wind gust to 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of intense thunderstorm cells rapidly formed in a strongly unstable air mass during the heat of the afternoon. The most intense cells merged into a small severe thunderstorm complex around the northern end of the Land Between The Lakes area (Kentucky Lake and Lake Barkley). This complex was associated with sporadic large hail and damaging winds from southern Livingston County eastward across Lyon and Caldwell Counties. The storms occurred ahead of a slow-moving weak cold front that extended from central Indiana to the Ozarks of southwest Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was over some roads in Wickliffe. A personal weather station in Blandville reported 1.95 inches in just over one hour, with the majority occurring within 30 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A heat wave brought three consecutive days of heat indices near or above 105 degrees. Excessive heat occurred over the Henderson/Owensboro region on the afternoons of the 27th and 28th, when heat indices peaked mostly from 109 to 115 degrees. Elsewhere, peak heat indices ranged mostly from 105 to 110 degrees on the 27th and 28th. On the 29th, the highest heat indices were from 100 to near 110 across west Kentucky. The heat and humidity occurred in a southwest wind flow ahead of a cold front that progressed southeast from the Plains over the three-day period.

Historic flash flooding struck parts of far western Kentucky. Among the hardest hit counties was Graves County, which had not completely recovered from a catastrophic tornado in December of 2021. The city of Mayfield, which bore the brunt of the December 2021 tornado, experienced catastrophic flooding that inundated dozens of homes, businesses, schools, and various other institutions. The governor of Kentucky visited the city of Mayfield to tour the damage. Some smaller communities suffered catastrophic damage, such as Arlington in Carlisle County, where multiple homes were flooded. Swiftly moving water through Arlington prompted water rescues from emergency services. Widespread flash flooding of roads made travel difficult or impossible for several hours during the peak of the heaviest rainfall in the morning. Among the most impactful road closures was Interstate 69 in southwest Graves County, near the community of Wingo. Other high-profile road closures included U.S. Highways 51, 62, and 45 at various locations in Ballard, McCracken, Graves, and Hickman Counties. Numerous water rescues were conducted for people stranded in homes and vehicles. Dozens of roads were washed out in several counties, including Ballard, Carlisle, Hickman, Fulton, and Graves. Swift water was across many roads, and water even overtopped a few bridges, including U.S. 60 between Paducah and Kevil. Crop damage was expected to run into the millions of dollars. At one farm alone in southern Graves County, crop damage was estimated around 40,000 dollars. The downtown area of Fulton was flooded, including at least one business. Widespread street flooding occurred in Murray, and at least one business was sandbagged. About a dozen businesses were flooded in Ballard County, along with another dozen residences. Widespread rainfall totals ranging from 6 to 12 inches occurred over the westernmost counties (west of Kentucky Lake), including the Paducah, Mayfield, and Murray areas. The majority of this rain fell within a 10-hour window between midnight and 10 AM. The highest confirmed rainfall total was 12.76 inches in Graves County, west of Mayfield near the small community of Fancy Farm. The Kentucky mesonet site six miles southwest of Mayfield received 11.28 of rain in a 24-hour period. Preliminary data indicates this broke the previous Kentucky state record for 24-hour rainfall. The old record was 10.48 in Louisville, KY back on March 1, 1997.||In addition to all the flooding, there were several reports of trees blown down across roads. A couple of large hail reports were also received.||The historic rainfall event was caused by a persistent thunderstorm complex that continually regenerated as it moved southeast across the region during the early morning hours of the 19th. This complex of thunderstorms was indirectly caused by the complex of severe thunderstorms during the midday hours of the 18th. The complex on the 18th left an outflow boundary from south central Missouri into northeast Arkansas. This boundary lifted back northeast into southeast Missouri during the overnight and early morning hours of July 19th. At the same time, a southwest low-level jet ramped up over the top of the surface boundary. Extremely high moisture levels were present, with precipitable water values up to 2.3 inches. The position of the boundary oriented parallel with the upper-level wind flow provided an ideal setup for training convection over the same areas. The initial storms blew up by 11 PM and continued to channel southeastward across the same areas through the following morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree 8 inches in diameter was blocking Highway 94.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small complex of thunderstorms tracked eastward across western Kentucky during the early evening hours. Sporadic tree damage and large hail accompanied the strongest storms, most of which followed a west-to-east corridor through Paducah to Princeton. The storm occurred ahead of a cold front that trailed southwest from the Great Lakes across central Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm complex moved east-southeast across western Kentucky. A thunderstorm along the leading edge of this complex produced locally damaging winds from the Paducah area eastward to the northern part of the Land-Between-The-Lakes region. The storms occurred along and ahead of a cold front that stretched from the Great Lakes region to the southern Plains.

A very hot and muggy day was underway on July 28th when strong thunderstorms developed in the Ohio Valley. A line of storms pressed into eastern Kentucky from the west that afternoon and yielded damaging wind gusts in Boyd and Greenup Counties. A 59 mph wind gust was observed at a Mesonet station in Cannonsburg as these storms passed through. The severe threat greatly diminished after the line of storms crossed east of the Ohio River.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Flatwoods, a tree fell down and blocked a local road. Another tree fell down onto the railroad tracks in Greenup.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of lines of thunderstorms moved northeast across far western Kentucky during the late afternoon and evening hours. The first line consisted of semi-discrete cells, one of which was associated with a weak tornado that tracked across the Kentucky Lake area. The second line was associated with a few isolated marginally severe wind gusts to 60 mph. The storms developed in response to a rather amplified 500 mb shortwave trough that progressed east into Iowa and Missouri by nightfall. With daytime heating, the atmosphere became very unstable in the afternoon. Mixed-layer capes were around 3000 j/kg late in the day. A belt of 35 to 45 knot mid-level west-southwesterly winds resulted in sufficient deep-layer wind shear for organized storm structures. An embedded tornado occurred in the vicinity of a residual outflow boundary arcing west from storms over central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were down near Hickman.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms quickly developed with strong daytime heating. The storms formed into loosely organized clusters, with locally damaging downbursts and isolated large hail. As the afternoon progressed, the storm clusters congealed into a rather large complex of storms with an organized cold pool. Strong gusty winds accompanied the leading edge of the cold pool gust front. The storms formed along and ahead of a cold front that trailed southwest from a low pressure center over northern Indiana. A 500 mb trough extending from the Great Lakes down across the Mississippi Valley provided additional support for storms. Moderately strong mid-level winds contributed to effective bulk wind shear around 35 knots, sufficient for some organization of the convection.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak disturbance moved over eastern Kentucky on July 2nd, bringing a broken line of showers and thunderstorms through early in the day. Additional activity sprouted up ahead of and along a cold front during the evening hours. Strong winds within one severe storm resulted in downed trees in Greenup County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two trees were blown down along State Route 1043 near South Shore.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm, muggy and unstable air mass was in place across eastern Kentucky on July 18th as a wavering front remained northwest of the Lower Ohio River. The first storm cluster of the day fired over central Kentucky during the early afternoon hours before moving into eastern Kentucky. This initial round of storms impacted areas primarily north of the Hal Rogers Parkway during the late afternoon and early evening. The combination of dry air in the mid-levels and sufficient shear led to a a couple of strong bowing line segments with estimated peak wind gusts in the 60 to 70 mph range. The most numerous instances of wind damage, primarily downed trees and power lines, were observed in Estill, Lee, Montgomery and Bath counties. A gust of 61 mph was recorded at the RAWS site close to the Jackson National Weather Service Office.
